preFix.title: Warp painting before weaving
preFix.subtitle: Dyeing a chained warp in sections to create colour-change fabric
preFix.excerpt: Painting the warp before beaming introduces colour changes that run perpendicular to the weft, producing a fabric where every pick reveals a new colour composition. The warp is chained off the warping board, laid flat, mordanted, painted in dye sections with a brush, heat-set, rinsed, and dried before being dressed onto the loom.
preFix.sourceNotes: Synthesised from established fibre-reactive and natural-dye practice for warp-painting. No single published source is followed verbatim; the mordanting chemistry, heat-setting times, and beaming sequence reflect common guild and dye-school practice documented across multiple published dye manuals and observation-led weaving instruction.

content.text: Warp painting works by introducing colour into the
warp

threads before they are beamed onto the loom. Each dye section is applied across a group of warp ends lying side by side in the chain, so as the cloth is woven, the colour changes flow diagonally or vertically through the fabric depending on the sett and the width of each painted section. The result is unlike anything achievable by dyeing weft yarn alone.

The technique works on any loom. It is particularly well-suited to a rigid heddle loom. The warp is narrow enough to be painted on a kitchen table. The relaxed sett also shows warp colour clearly in the finished cloth.
What you need

attrs.name: Rigid heddle loom, 40 cm or wider
Warp yarn: undyed wool or cotton, as planned for the project
Fibre-reactive dye powder (for cotton) or acid dye (for wool), two to four colours
Mordant: alum for wool, or soda ash for cotton
Plastic cling film, enough to wrap the warp chain twice
Rubber or latex gloves and eye protection

content.text: Step 1: Wind and mordant the warp
Wind the warp on the warping board to the planned length and width. Tie the cross securely with contrasting yarn and remove the chained warp. Before painting, the fibre must be
mordanted
so the dye bonds permanently.

For wool warp with acid dyes: dissolve alum (potassium aluminium sulphate) in hot water at 15% weight of fibre. Submerge the wetted warp chain and simmer for 45 minutes, then cool in the bath. Squeeze out (do not wring) and lay flat for painting while still damp.

For cotton warp with fibre-reactive dyes: dissolve soda ash at 30 g per litre of warm water. Submerge the wetted warp chain for 20 minutes, squeeze out, and lay flat. Soda-ash-treated cotton does not need further soaking; begin painting immediately.
Step 2: Lay the warp flat and plan the colour sections

Cover a flat table with plastic sheeting or bin bags. Lay the warp chain out along the table, spreading the threads apart gently so they lie as a flat band rather than a rope. Tie loosely at intervals to keep the threads spread.

Decide on the colour sections before picking up a brush. Each section will produce a stripe of colour running diagonally across the woven cloth. Sections of 15-20 cm on the warp produce colour changes every few centimetres of woven cloth at a typical rigid heddle sett of 8 epi (approximately 3 ends per cm). Mark the section boundaries with loose ties or chalk marks on the table covering.
Step 3: Paint the dye sections

Mix dye solutions to the manufacturer's instructions. Apply each colour with a brush, working from the back of the warp chain through to the front so both faces of the flat band are saturated. Where two colours meet, paint a narrow blending zone by pulling a small amount of each colour into the other with the brush tip. This prevents a hard line that can look abrupt in the woven cloth.
Lift each section of the chain and paint the underside as well. Unpainted threads show as white lines in the cloth.
Step 4: Heat-set the dye
Once the full warp chain is painted, roll it loosely inside a sheet of cling film, coiling the roll if the warp is long. The cling film keeps moisture in during
heat-setting

Acid dyes on wool: place the wrapped roll on a baking tray and set the oven to 150 C. Leave for 30-40 minutes. The dye fixes by acid and heat together; the alum mordant assists bonding. Cool before unwrapping.

Fibre-reactive dyes on cotton: oven heat at 150 C for 30 minutes will fix fibre-reactive dyes; alternatively, leave the wrapped roll at room temperature for 24 hours (cold batch). The soda-ash mordanting done in Step 1 provides the alkaline environment needed for fixing without steam.

Natural dyes on alum-mordanted wool: steam over a pot of simmering water for 45-60 minutes rather than oven-setting. Natural dye chemistry needs both heat and moisture to fix, so steaming is more reliable than dry oven heat.
Step 5: Rinse, dry, and beam

Rinse the warp chain in cool water until the water runs clear. Squeeze out gently and hang to dry without stretching. Once dry, re-open the chain into a single layer, locate the cross, and dress the loom as normal.

Handle the warp carefully during beaming: the warp threads are individual after drying and can shed slightly if the chain has felted at all (wool is at some risk). Use a raddle or a reed to spread threads evenly and beam under consistent tension.
How the warp colour reads in the woven cloth

At a balanced plain-weave sett, the warp and weft are equally visible. A neutral weft lets the warp painting dominate. A weft that matches one of the painted warp colours will blend in wherever that colour appears in the warp, producing a fabric that shifts from solid blocks to striped areas as the painted sections change. A weft colour that contrasts with all the warp colours will read as a subtle horizontal stripe overlaid on the warp colour sections.

The width of each colour section on the warp governs how quickly the colour changes as weaving progresses. A 10 cm section at 8 epi (3 ends per cm) across a 40 cm warp width will have all the threads of that colour pass through the cloth within approximately 10 cm of weaving length. Broader sections (30-40 cm) create long colour runs; very narrow sections (5 cm or less) create a confetti-like mixed effect.

attrs.fix: During painting, lift and turn the chain to access the underside. The chain should be laid as flat as possible so the brush can reach all layers.
attrs.cause: Parts of the warp chain were not penetrated by the dye during painting
attrs.problem: White lines appear in the woven cloth

attrs.fix: Squeeze out excess mordant solution before painting. Allow each colour to absorb slightly before painting the adjacent section. Keeping colours at least 2 cm apart reduces bleeding.
attrs.cause: The warp was too wet when painted, or adjacent colours were applied while the previous colour was still very fluid
attrs.problem: Dye bleeds across colour boundaries and the sections merge into muddy colours

attrs.fix: Check that the correct mordant was used for the fibre type (alum for protein fibres, soda ash for cellulose). Extend heat-setting time by 10-15 minutes and ensure the internal temperature of the wrapped roll actually reaches the required level.
attrs.cause: Insufficient mordanting, under-fixation time, or the wrong dye type for the fibre
attrs.problem: The colour washes out significantly in the first rinse after heat-setting
